title: Production roles and craft skills
summary: This lesson deconstructs the IB Film SL Portfolio, focusing on how to effectively document production roles and craft skills. It provides a detailed guide on meeting the assessment criteria by linking filmmaker intentions to the practical processes of filmmaking and justifying creative choices with analytical precision.
learningObjectives: Identify and articulate the specific responsibilities of the three chosen production roles for the film portfolio.
Analyse how specific craft skills (e.g., cinematography, editing, sound design) are used to realise filmmaker intentions.
Document the filmmaking process with clear, relevant, and insightful evidence of skill acquisition and problem-solving.
Justify creative and technical choices using precise film language, connecting them to context, theory, and intended meaning.
Critically reflect on the successes and challenges of the production process, evaluating the impact of choices on the final film.

simpleExplanation.title: The Director's Diary: Crafting Your Portfolio
simpleExplanation.summary: The Film Portfolio isn't just about the finished film; it's the story of how you made it. This task requires you to act as a one-person film crew, mastering three key production roles and documenting your journey from idea to final cut. Examiners want to see your thought process, your problem-solving, and your growing mastery of film language.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your portfolio as a chef's detailed recipe journal for a signature dish. The final film is the dish itself, but the journal (your portfolio) is where the real craft is revealed. It contains the initial concept (filmmaker intentions), the ingredient sourcing and prep work (pre-production), the cooking process with notes on technique (production), and the final plating and seasoning (post-production/editing). A great journal doesn't just list the steps; it explains *why* a certain spice was chosen or *why* a specific cooking time was used, proving the chef's expertise.
steps: Step 1: Define Your Vision and Roles. Clearly articulate your filmmaker intentions on page one and explicitly state the three production roles you will document (e.g., Director, Cinematographer, Editor).

Step 2: Gather Evidence Systematically. For each role, collect visual and written proof of your work at every stage: annotated scripts, storyboards, lighting diagrams, screengrabs of your editing timeline, sound mixing notes.

Step 3: Justify, Don't Describe. Move beyond stating *what* you did. Use your portfolio pages to explain *why* you made each choice, linking it back to your intentions and using precise film terminology.

Step 4: Reflect on the Process. In your commentary, demonstrate self-awareness. Discuss challenges you faced, how you overcame them, and how your skills developed. Evaluate the effectiveness of your choices in the final product.

flashcards.back: A formal statement outlining the film's purpose, themes, target audience, and intended stylistic and emotional impact. It is the guiding principle against which all creative choices are justified in the portfolio.
For the SL portfolio, students must document their work in THREE of the following five roles: Director, Writer, Cinematographer, Editor, Sound Designer.
The specific technical and creative abilities associated with a production role, such as framing and composition (Cinematographer), pacing and rhythm (Editor), or dialogue and subtext (Writer).
Explaining the 'why' behind a creative choice, linking it to filmmaker intentions, genre conventions, theoretical concepts, or specific influences. This is a higher-order skill than description.
The collection of evidence (e.g., storyboards, annotated scripts, production stills, timeline screenshots) that illustrates the student's work and skill development in their chosen roles.
The role responsible for the overall creative vision and interpretation of the script, guiding the actors and technical crew to realise this vision on screen.
The role responsible for the visual realisation of the film, including decisions on lighting, framing, camera movement, and lens choice to create a specific mood and meaning.
The role responsible for assembling the raw footage, shaping the film's structure, pacing, and rhythm to create a coherent and emotionally impactful narrative.
The role responsible for the entire auditory landscape of the film, including dialogue, sound effects (foley), ambient sound, and music, and how they are mixed to support the narrative.
The role responsible for creating the screenplay, including the plot, characters, dialogue, and formatting. In the portfolio, this includes drafts and annotations showing development.

Description: 'I used a close-up.' Justification: 'I employed a tight close-up to isolate the character, forcing the audience to confront their internal turmoil, which supports my intention of creating an uncomfortable intimacy.'
A key assessment point in Criterion B. It means showing evidence of learning, problem-solving, and improvement throughout the production process, not just demonstrating pre-existing ability.

faq.q: Can I get help from friends or professionals on my film?
faq.a: Yes, but with strict limitations. The IB's academic integrity policy allows for collaboration, but the work for your three chosen and documented production roles must be entirely your own. For example, you can have a friend act in your film or hold a boom mic, but if one of your roles is 'Cinematographer', you must be the one operating the camera and making all the creative decisions about lighting and framing. Your portfolio must be an honest reflection of *your* work and *your* skills.

faq.q: What if my final film isn't very good or didn't turn out how I planned?
faq.a: This is a common and valuable part of the learning process. The portfolio is your chance to analyse *why* it didn't meet your intentions. A high-scoring portfolio can be based on a film with technical flaws, as long as the student demonstrates self-awareness, reflects critically on the challenges, and provides insightful analysis of what they learned. Honesty and reflection are rewarded more than a deceptively 'perfect' product.

faq.q: How should I choose my three production roles?
faq.a: Choose roles that genuinely interest you and that form a logical combination. A common and effective trio is Writer, Director, and Editor, as it follows the core creative spine of a project from conception to final form. Another strong combination is Director, Cinematographer, and Editor. Avoid choosing roles where it might be difficult to generate distinct evidence. Ultimately, your passion and engagement with the roles will be evident in the depth and quality of your documentation.

faq.q: How many pages should I dedicate to each role?
faq.a: With 9 pages total, and the first page for your intentions, you have 8 pages to cover three roles. A balanced approach is best. Aim for roughly 2-3 pages per role. You might have 3 pages for a major role like Director and 2.5 for two others. The key is to ensure each role is given sufficient space to present clear evidence and justification. Do not neglect any of your three chosen roles.

sections.content: Welcome to the core practical component of IB Film: the Film Portfolio. This is your opportunity to synthesise theory with practice. Success in this task is not merely about producing a polished film, but about meticulously documenting your journey as a filmmaker. The portfolio is an academic and creative document that showcases your ability to make intentional, informed, and reflective decisions across three distinct production roles. This lesson will equip you to move beyond simple description and into the high-level justification and analysis that examiners reward.
Understanding the Portfolio's Structure and Purpose

The SL Film Portfolio consists of two parts: a finished film (3-5 minutes) and a 9-page portfolio document. The document is not a production diary; it is a curated, analytical justification of your work. You must select and provide evidence for three production roles from the following list: Director, Writer, Cinematographer, Editor, and Sound Designer. Your 9 pages must be strategically allocated to demonstrate your engagement and skill acquisition across all three chosen roles.

items: The portfolio is assessed on three criteria: Coherent Presentation and Filmmaker Intentions (A), Documentation of Process and Acquisition of Skills (B), and Justification of Creative Choices (C).
Your first page should almost always be your 'Filmmaker Intentions' statement, as it provides the framework for the entire project.
Each subsequent page should focus on providing specific, annotated evidence for your chosen roles.
The portfolio and the film are assessed holistically; a strong portfolio can explain and contextualise imperfections in the final film.

sections.question: Write a sample 'Filmmaker Intentions' statement for a short film titled 'Echoes', which explores the theme of memory and loss.
sections.solution:
**Filmmaker Intentions: 'Echoes'**

My intention with 'Echoes' is to create a poignant, non-linear narrative that explores the fragmented nature of memory following a significant loss. The film will target an audience familiar with art-house cinema, inviting interpretation rather than providing simple answers. My primary goal is to evoke a feeling of melancholic nostalgia and the disorienting sensation of a memory fading over time.

To achieve this, I will employ several key strategies. As **Writer**, I will structure the script around recurring motifs and fragmented dialogue, avoiding a traditional three-act structure. As **Cinematographer**, I will utilise a shallow depth of field and a desaturated, cool-toned colour palette to visually isolate the protagonist within his own mind and represent the emotional coldness of his grief. Camera movement will be minimal and deliberate, favouring slow, lingering tracking shots that mimic the process of searching through one's memories. As **Editor**, I will use J-cuts and L-cuts to blur the lines between past and present, creating a seamless flow between different time periods and reinforcing the film's central theme that memory is a fluid, ever-present force.

sections.content: Criterion B: Documenting Process and Acquiring Skills

This criterion is the heart of your portfolio. It assesses your ability to provide concrete evidence of your work and, crucially, your learning. Top-band responses show a clear progression of ideas and demonstrate problem-solving. It is vital to show the 'before' and 'after'—the initial plan (storyboard, script excerpt) and the final result (film still, screenshot), accompanied by commentary explaining the journey between the two.

items: **Show, Don't Tell:** Instead of saying 'I learned how to light a scene', include a lighting diagram you designed, a production still showing the setup, and an annotation explaining how it solved a specific problem (e.g., 'The initial setup created harsh shadows, so I added a fill light to soften the contrast and better reflect the character's gentle nature').
**Visuals are Key:** Your portfolio should be visually rich. Use screenshots of your editing timeline, colour grading panels, audio mixing software, and annotated script pages.

**Annotate Everything:** Do not assume the examiner will understand the significance of an image. Use captions and annotations to explain what the evidence shows, how it relates to your role, and how it connects to your intentions.

**Embrace Challenges:** Documenting a problem and how you solved it (or attempted to) is powerful evidence of skill acquisition. For example, 'The diegetic sound recorded on location was unusable due to wind noise. As Sound Designer, I recreated the soundscape using foley and sourced ambient tracks, which ultimately gave me more control over the mood.'

sections.content: Create a 'portfolio folder' on your computer from day one. Every time you create a storyboard, write a script draft, take a test shot, or make a significant edit, save a version or a screenshot into this folder with a descriptive filename. This will make assembling your final 9 pages infinitely easier than trying to recreate your process after the fact.

If Criterion B is the 'how', Criterion C is the 'why'. This is where you elevate your portfolio from a report to an analysis. Here, you must connect your practical decisions to a wider understanding of film. This involves using precise terminology and linking your choices to genre, theory, specific filmmakers (influences), or the desired psychological effect on the audience. Every justification must loop back to your stated filmmaker intentions.

sections.question: Model a portfolio page section documenting the role of 'Editor', justifying the choice of a specific cutting technique.
sections.solution:
**Portfolio Page 5: The Role of the Editor - Crafting Disorientation**

**Justification of Jump Cuts:**

[*Include a screenshot of the editing timeline showing a sequence with several rapid cuts within the same shot.*]

**Annotation:** In the scene where the protagonist experiences a panic attack, my intention was to visually disrupt the viewer's sense of time and space, mirroring his psychological distress. To achieve this, I deliberately broke the 30-degree rule and employed a series of jump cuts (see timeline, 01:32:04 - 01:35:11). This technique, famously used by Jean-Luc Godard in 'À bout de souffle' to create a sense of unease and modernity, serves a similar purpose here. By cutting between fractions of the same continuous take, I fragment the character's movement and create a jarring, stuttering rhythm. This editorial choice directly supports my filmmaker intention to create a visceral and disorienting audience experience, moving beyond simple narrative progression to convey a purely emotional state. The resulting sequence is intentionally uncomfortable, denying the viewer the smooth continuity they expect and forcing them into the protagonist's fractured perspective.

sections.content: The most sophisticated portfolios demonstrate a clear understanding of how the three chosen roles inform one another. Your portfolio should not feel like three separate, siloed reports. Show how a decision made as a Writer influenced your choices as a Cinematographer, and how both were ultimately shaped by your work as an Editor. This holistic approach demonstrates a mature understanding of the collaborative and interconnected nature of filmmaking.

items: **Example Connection:** 'As **Writer**, I wrote a scene with no dialogue to emphasise the character's isolation. This presented a challenge and opportunity for me as **Director** to focus purely on visual storytelling. Consequently, as **Cinematographer**, I designed a shot list using wide, empty frames to reinforce this theme, a choice that was then held for longer durations in my role as **Editor** to create a contemplative pace.'

Use your page layout to create connections. You could place a script excerpt (Writer) next to a storyboard (Director) and a final film still (Cinematographer) to show the evolution of a single moment across your roles.

Reflect on moments where your roles were in conflict. For instance, 'As Editor, I realised that a line of dialogue I had written as Writer was redundant, as the actor's performance, which I guided as Director, already conveyed the emotion. I therefore cut the line to strengthen the principle of 'show, don't tell'.'
